original_abstract: Publicly available scientific resources provide the foundation for establishing evaluation standards, enhancing educational platforms, and ensuring experimental reproducibility. Version 4 of the Insight ToolKit (ITKv4) introduces a significant evolution in image registration methodology, aiming to set new open-source standards for the community. Compared to its predecessors, ITKv4 offers several key technical innovations, including support for multivariate images and objective functions. A primary advancement is the unification of high-dimensional (deformation field) and low-dimensional (affine) transformations. This is achieved through metrics that remain reusable across different transform types and the implementation of composite transforms, which allow users to chain arbitrary sequences of geometric mappings seamlessly. To improve efficiency, metrics and optimizers are now designed to fully exploit multi-core hardware. Furthermore, ITKv4 minimizes the burden of parameter tuning by introducing principled heuristics that automatically calculate scaling across disparate parameter types, such as rotations and translations, while also constraining step sizes for gradient-based optimization. Consequently, researchers can focus more on designing and comparing registration strategies rather than manual tuning. Ultimately, the ITKv4 framework serves as a robust structure for reproducible research and provides an extensive foundation for evaluating emerging registration techniques. We conclude by contextualizing these developments through a reference registration evaluation study applied to the labeling of pediatric brain images.
humanized_abstract: Data from the open scientific literature serves as the basis for the definition of a set of evaluation criteria, as well as the construction of teaching tools and data sets for validation. We present Version 4 of the Insight ToolKit (ITKv4) that brings about a leap in the image registration methodology with the aim of setting a new standard in the open source community. As compared to previous versions of ITK, several important technical advances are introduced: multi-variate images and objective functions are supported; high dimensional (deformation field) and low dimensional (affine) transforms are unified through reusable metrics defined on the infinite dimensional space of transforms, as well as through composable transforms that allow for a seamless combination of any number of geometric transforms. Additional advances include: multi-processor support for metrics and optimizers, more efficient parameter management using principled heuristics for choosing scaling factors among parameters having vastly different scales (e.g. rotations and translations), as compared to Others), and constrained step sizes for gradient-based optimization. With the advances in the ITKv4 framework, researchers can spend more time on the construction and comparison of registration algorithms. We demonstrate the new functionalities of the ITK framework through a reference evaluation study of registrations of a large data set of labeled pediatric brain images.
